requirements.description:
The RTF formatter's format_unencoded method currently closes the document
      by writing a bare closing brace ("}") with no trailing newline. All RTF
      formatter tests use result.endswith(...foot) where foot = r'\par' + '\n' + '}' + '\n',
      which requires the document to end with a newline character after the closing brace.
      Currently the output ends with "}" instead of "}\n", causing all endswith
      assertions to fail.
requirements.contract:
The format_unencoded method MUST write a trailing newline character ('
')
      after the final closing brace "}" that ends the RTF document. The resulting
      output must end with the sequence "\par
}
" for non-empty input, and with
      "}
" in all cases. No other aspect of the formatter's output should change.

requirements.description:
The RtfFormatter class is missing a publicly accessible class-level method
      hex_to_rtf_color. Tests call RtfFormatter.hex_to_rtf_color(hex_string) directly
      as a type-level call (not on an instance). This causes AttributeError in 6 tests
      that use this utility to validate that color table entries are correctly formed.
      Additionally, this method is required internally by the linenos and hl_lines
      features (requirements 3 and 4) to convert hex color option values into RTF
      color table entry strings.
requirements.contract:
Add a static method (or class method) hex_to_rtf_color to RtfFormatter.

      Signature: hex_to_rtf_color(hex_color: str) -> str

      Input: a hex color string, optionally prefixed with "#" (e.g. "abcdef" or
      "#abcdef"). The meaningful content is 6 hex characters representing RGB.

      Output: an RTF color table entry string of the form \redR\greenG\blueB; where
      R, G, B are decimal integers (0–255) derived from the respective hex pairs.

      Example: hex_to_rtf_color("abcdef") → r'\red171\green205\blue239;'
      Example: hex_to_rtf_color("#123456") → r'\red18\green52\blue86;'

      The method must be callable as RtfFormatter.hex_to_rtf_color(s) without
      instantiating the class. Both forms (with and without leading "#") must be
      accepted.

requirements.description:
The RTF formatter does not support line numbering. Tests for linenos, linenostart,
      linenostep, lineno_padding, lineno_color, and lineno_fontsize options all fail
      because those options are not recognized or handled. When linenos=True, each
      output line must be prefixed with a formatted line-number span using the first
      color table entry (\cf1) and padded with spaces. The formatter also needs to
      handle hl_lines (highlighted lines) by wrapping matching lines in a \highlight
      color group, and support the hl_linenostart option that shifts highlight line
      numbering to match linenostart.
requirements.contract:
Extend RtfFormatter.__init__ to accept and store the following options:
        - linenos (bool, default False): enable line number display
        - lineno_padding (int, default 0): extra spaces after line number
        - linenostart (int, default 1): starting line number
        - linenostep (int, default 1): only display numbers on multiples of this step;
          blank space placeholder for skipped lines
        - lineno_color (str, optional hex): override line number color; falls back to
          the Style.line_number_color attribute, and if that is "inherit" falls back to
          the _ansimap['ansibrightblack'] color
        - lineno_fontsize (int, optional): font size for line numbers in half-points
        - hl_lines (str, default ""): space-separated line numbers to highlight
        - hl_color (str, optional hex): override highlight background color; falls back
          to Style.highlight_color
        - hl_linenostart (bool, default False): when True, treat hl_lines numbers as
          absolute line numbers starting from linenostart

      When linenos=True:
        - The line number color MUST be the FIRST entry in the RTF color table (\cf1)
        - Each output line must be prefixed with: {\cf1 N  } where N is the right-aligned
          line number padded on the left with spaces so that the field width equals
          len(str(last_line_number)), followed by lineno_padding spaces
        - If linenostep > 1 and the current line number is not a multiple of
          linenostep, output blank spaces instead of the number

      When hl_lines is non-empty:
        - If linenos=True, the highlight color MUST be the SECOND entry in the color
          table; if linenos=False, it MUST be the FIRST entry
        - Each highlighted line (including its \par) must be wrapped:
          {\highlightN content\par}
        - If hl_linenostart=True, treat the numbers in hl_lines as absolute line
          numbers; otherwise treat them as 1-based offsets from the start of the text

      The format of output lines must follow: [line_number_prefix][content]\par\n
      with highlighted lines wrapped: {\highlightN [line_number_prefix][content]\par}\n

      The full output for test_linenos_python must match the expected header/body/footer
      format that includes the color table entries on separate lines, followed by \f0\sa0,
      \dntblnsbdb, content lines, and the closing } on its own line with trailing newline.

requirements.description:
There is no MojoLexer in the codebase. Tests for Mojo snippets fail with
      "pygments.util.ClassNotFound: no lexer for alias 'mojo' found", and related
      basic API tests are not collected because the class does not exist.
      Mojo is a systems programming language with Python-like syntax extended with
      fn declarations, struct/trait definitions, and specific keyword handling.
requirements.contract:
Create a MojoLexer class in code/pygments/lexers/mojo.py with:
        - name = 'Mojo'
        - aliases = ['mojo']
        - filenames = ['*.mojo']

      The lexer must produce the following token types for the given constructs:
        Keywords (Keyword token): fn, struct, class, elif, else, with, match, assert,
          and, or, not, in, is, pass, break, continue, return, yield, raise, del, lambda,
          import, from, as, global, nonlocal, try, except, finally, for, while, async,
          await, decorator-related keywords
        Name.Function token: def (context-sensitive: treated as identifier/Name.Function
          when appearing standalone without a following name)
        Name.Struct token: trait (when appearing standalone)
        Name.Class token: if (when appearing standalone as soft keyword)
        Keyword.Constant: True, False, None
        Name.Builtin: print and other Python built-ins
        Literal.Number.Integer: decimal integers with optional single underscores as
          separators (e.g. 5_6 valid, 5__6 invalid — double underscore is not a numeric
          separator); hexadecimal integers (0x...)
        Literal.Number.Float: floats matching digit.digit, .digit, digit., digit e digit
          patterns; exponent notation (e.g. 2e1, 11.2e-3)
        Operator: arithmetic, comparison, assignment operators including @, +=, =, -, +, .
        Punctuation: (, ), [, ], {, }, :, ,
        Literal.String.Double / Literal.String: double-quoted strings
        Literal.String.Affix: f-string prefix f before a string literal
        Literal.String.Interpol: { and } delimiters within f-strings
        Comment.Single: # line comments
        Text.Whitespace: spaces, tabs, newlines

      Register the lexer in _mapping.py:
        'MojoLexer': ('pygments.lexers.mojo', 'Mojo', ('mojo',), ('*.mojo',), ())

      The lexer must also handle the examplefiles/mojo/sample.mojo without generating
      Error tokens.

requirements.description:
There is no SoongLexer in the codebase. Tests for Soong snippets fail with
      "pygments.util.ClassNotFound: no lexer for alias 'soong' found", and related
      basic API tests are not collected. Soong is the Android build system language
      (Blueprint files) with a JSON-like syntax supporting C++-style comments,
      module declarations, property assignments, lists, maps, and operators.
requirements.contract:
Create a SoongLexer class in code/pygments/lexers/soong.py with:
        - name = 'Soong'
        - aliases = ['soong']
        - filenames = [] (no standard file extension; Blueprint files can be added if desired)

      The lexer must produce the following token types:
        Name.Function: bare identifiers used as module type names at the start of a
          block (e.g. "package", "module_name" before "{")
        Name.Variable: bare identifiers used as top-level variable names on the left
          side of "=" or "+=" assignments
        Name: bare identifiers used as property names (key in key: value pairs inside
          blocks) or as unquoted value references
        Operator: =, +=
        Punctuation: {, }, [, ], :, ,
        Literal.String: double-quoted string literals (entire quoted string as one token)
        Literal.Number.Integer: decimal integers
        Literal.Number.Hex: hexadecimal integers (0x...)
        Comment.Single: // line comments
        Comment.Multiline: /* ... */ block comments
        Text.Whitespace: spaces, tabs, and newline sequences (including indentation)

      Key behavioral details from test snippets:
        - Module declarations: an identifier followed by "{" — the identifier is Name.Function
        - Variable assignments at top level: identifier before "=" or "+=" is Name.Variable
        - Property names inside a block (identifier before ":") are Name
        - Unquoted identifiers used as values (not before ":" or "{") are Name
        - Nested blocks are allowed; inner block property names follow the same rules
        - "package" at the start of a block declaration tokenizes as Name.Function

      Register the lexer in _mapping.py:
        'SoongLexer': ('pygments.lexers.soong', 'Soong', ('soong',), (), ())
